rows.title: Nebraska LB 1083: Legislative Bill on Data Privacy Introduced in Nebraska Legislature
rows.summary:
Nebraska Legislative Bill 1083 (LB 1083) is a bill introduced in the Nebraska Legislature, the unicameral state legislature of Nebraska, United States, addressing data privacy requirements applicable within the state of Nebraska.

On introduction in the Nebraska Legislature, LB 1083 was referred to the relevant standing committee for consideration. The bill forms part of a broader legislative effort in Nebraska to establish or expand statutory protections for consumers regarding the collection, processing, and use of personal data by businesses operating in the state.

The bill, as introduced, sets out obligations for data controllers and processors doing business in Nebraska, including requirements relating to consumer rights such as the right to access, correct, delete, and opt out of the processing of personal data for purposes including targeted advertising and the sale of personal data. The Legislature determined that entities meeting defined thresholds of data processing activity or revenue would fall within the scope of the bill's provisions. Specific article-level details, including precise thresholds, exemptions, and enforcement mechanisms, are contained in the enrolled text of the bill as advanced through the legislative process.

Nebraska enacted the Nebraska Data Privacy Act (NDPA) under LB 1074 in 2024, and LB 1083 may represent a subsequent or companion measure amending, supplementing, or extending those earlier provisions. The Nebraska Legislature's official legislative tracking system, the Nebraska Legislature website, provides the authoritative text and legislative history of LB 1083, including committee hearings, amendments, and any floor debate transcripts. Enforcement authority under Nebraska's data privacy framework resides with the Nebraska Attorney General, who is empowered to investigate violations and bring civil actions against non-compliant entities. No private right of action is provided under the existing Nebraska data privacy statutory framework.

rows.title: New Brunswick Ombud Submits 2025 Legislative Review Memorandum on Right to Information and Privacy Protection Act
rows.summary:
The Office of the Ombud of New Brunswick has submitted a formal memorandum to the Minister of Finance and Treasury Board calling for amendments to the Right to Information and Protection of Privacy Act (RIPA/LDIPVP) across definitions, oversight powers, artificial intelligence governance and exceptions to disclosure.

On 12 September 2025, Ombud Marie-France Pelletier addressed her memorandum to the Honourable René Legacy, Minister of Finance and Treasury Board, in her capacity as the designated statutory oversight body under s. 64.1(1) of the Act. The memorandum sets out recommendations arising from the Office's supervisory experience under the LDIPVP and from research into best practices observed across other Canadian provinces and territories. No fines are associated with this submission.

The Ombud identifies three areas for reform. Part 1 addresses existing statutory provisions, and the Ombud recommends that the definition of "government body" be expanded, that the definition of "personal information" be broadened, and that definitions relating to artificial intelligence be introduced into the Act. The Office also recommends amendments to response timelines under Article 11, refinements to the power to disregard requests under Article 15, including resolution of inconsistencies between English and French text, and revised treatment of mandatory exceptions relating to Executive Council confidences and historical Cabinet documents under Article 17. The Ombud finds that the current framework for third-party privacy protections under Article 21 and commercial interest exceptions under Article 22 also warrant revision.

Part 2 addresses the General Regulation, with the Ombud recommending that the definition of privacy breach be expanded to capture lost or stolen information under Article 4.2, and that the Office be notified of all court recourses and appeals under Articles 5 and 7, including a formal appeal procedure initiated by the Ombud. Part 3 raises broader policy matters: the Office recommends that it be consulted on draft legislation capable of affecting access or privacy rights, that it be granted intervener status in recourse and appeal proceedings, and that it receive recommendation or order-making powers. The memorandum further identifies obligations to document decisions involving artificial intelligence and recommends introduction of a public interest override provision governing exceptions to disclosure.
rows.body_name: Office of the Access to Information and Privacy Commissioner (New Brunswick)
